topic: What actually breaks first in an off-grid garden system
voiceover_script: After enough hours running, something in this system breaks first. Here's what. It's never the Raspberry Pi. It's almost always something touching water or dirt. Drippers block first — one flake of algae in an unfiltered line and a whole bed stops getting water. Soil sensors go next, usually from being pulled loose rather than actually failing — a re-seat fixes most of them. Batteries fade slowest but hit hardest — a shaded panel or a worn cell shows up as a flat battery every morning. Know the order things fail in, and every fix is a five minute job, not a rebuild. The full troubleshooting guide covers all of it, symptom to fix — link in bio.

scenes.text: After enough hours running, something in this system breaks first. Here's what.
It's never the Raspberry Pi. It's almost always something touching water or dirt.
Drippers block first — one flake of algae in an unfiltered line and a whole bed stops getting water.
Soil sensors go next, usually from being pulled loose rather than actually failing — a re-seat fixes most of them.
Batteries fade slowest but hit hardest — a shaded panel or a worn cell shows up as a flat battery every morning.
Know the order things fail in, and every fix is a five minute job, not a rebuild.
The full troubleshooting guide covers all of it, symptom to fix — link in bio.
